title: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine

items.subtopic: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY
items.title: Importance of Intensity in Motor Recovery Training Post-Stroke
items.content: Recent randomized controlled clinical trials have highlighted the critical role of training intensity for sustained long-term motor improvements after a stroke. This underscores the importance of high-intensity regimens in neurologic rehabilitation to harness activity-dependent plasticity mechanisms.
keyPoints: Intensity of training is crucial for sustained long-term improvements post-stroke.
Activity-dependent plasticity mechanisms are key targets in neurologic rehabilitation.
Emerging technologies can enhance access, adherence, and engagement in high-intensity regimens.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY

items.subtopic: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY
items.title: Harnessing Neural Plasticity for Functional Restoration Across Domains
items.content: Neurologic rehabilitation technologies aim to utilize neural plasticity mechanisms across various functional domains such as movement, sensory processing, language, pain, and cognition. These approaches are supported by evidence from rodent and nonhuman primate models indicating that cortical motor map plasticity and coordinated neuronal firing contribute to observed improvements.
keyPoints: Neural plasticity can be harnessed across diverse functional domains including movement, sensory processing, language, pain, and cognition.
Cortical 'motor maps' plasticity and coordinated neuronal firing are underlying mechanisms for rehabilitation success.
Emerging technologies have the potential to revolutionize care by increasing access and engagement.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY

items.title: Role of Rehabilitation Robotics in Stroke Recovery
items.content: Rehabilitation robotics, including upper and lower limb devices, can enhance motor outcomes post-stroke by providing high-intensity movement practice beyond standard care. These systems measure precise movement parameters and offer quantitative feedback, aiding neural plasticity through patient engagement and attentional pathways.
keyPoints: Robotic therapies focus on high-intensity movement practice surpassing existing standards of care.
Systems provide precise measurement of kinematics and quantitative performance feedback.
Engagement and recruitment of attentional/reward pathways drive neural plasticity.
Antigravity support allows task engagement even with severe weakness.
Recent analysis suggests robotic devices may match or exceed outcomes of standard care.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · ROBOTICS

items.title: Implementation Challenges and Future Directions in Robotic Rehabilitation
items.content: While rehabilitation robotics show promise, their implementation outside clinical trials is challenging due to costs and effort. The potential for robotic devices to complement conventional therapy is supported by studies showing similar gains with both methods. Future research should focus on optimizing adaptive and quantitative methods to enhance recovery.
keyPoints: Intensive training delivery is a challenge from healthcare system and patient perspectives.
Robotic rehabilitation can be costly and difficult to maintain outside clinical trials.
A randomized trial showed robotic therapy matched conventional therapy in improving function for chronic stroke patients with moderate-to-severe deficits over an 8-week period.
Future work should define optimal use of adaptive and quantitative methods in robotics.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · ROBOTICS

items.title: VR and AR for Neurological Rehabilitation: Tailored Interventions and Monitoring
items.content: Virtual reality (VR) provides a controlled environment where patients can interact with simulated realities, allowing precise monitoring of clinically relevant parameters such as motor actions, perception, and cognitive processing. This enables tailored rehabilitation training that maximizes engagement and motivation.
keyPoints: VR allows dynamic interaction in a precisely controlled environment.
Monitoring includes measuring motor actions, perception, and cognitive processing.
Tailored VR environments can maximize patient engagement and task difficulty.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.title: AR for Enhancing Environmental Interaction in Neurological Impairments
items.content: Augmented reality (AR) overlays artificial filters on the physical world, enhancing environmental interaction for patients with neurologic impairments. It is particularly beneficial for stroke rehabilitation by providing enriched experiences that can improve motor and cognitive outcomes.
keyPoints: AR provides an enhanced view of the actual physical environment.
It offers unique rehabilitation interventions for stroke patients.
Environmental enrichment through AR may improve motor and cognitive outcomes.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.title: AR-Based Therapy for Phantom Limb Pain: Innovative Treatment Approach
items.content: Augmented reality (AR) offers a novel treatment for phantom limb pain by enabling 'phantom motor execution' through machine-learning algorithms. This approach decodes movements from EMG activity at the stump, projecting inferred movements onto an AR screen to reduce pain.
keyPoints: AR-based therapy uses machine learning to decode phantom limb movements.
EMG activity at the stump predicts distal limb movements.
Patients with long-term refractory phantom limb pain can experience significant pain reduction.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.title: Neurogaming as a Therapeutic Tool for Cognitive Deficits
items.content: Computerized video games, or neurogames, have demonstrated potential in improving cognitive deficits related to aging and neuropsychiatric disorders. These games adaptively target specific neurocognitive domains such as speed of processing, multitasking, and distraction suppression by adjusting difficulty levels based on the user's performance.
keyPoints: Neurogames can ameliorate visual perception, age-related degeneration, and neuropsychiatric disorders.
Adaptive challenges focus on specific cognitive domains like speed of processing and interference training.
Research shows neuroplasticity at both microscale (single neuron spiking) and macroscale (EEG-based event-related potentials).
Evidence from small-sample RCTs is promising, but larger RCTs are needed for definitive therapeutic benefit.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROGAMING

items.title: Future Directions in Neurogaming: Enhancing Generalizability and Efficacy
items.content: Next-generation neurogames aim to improve generalizability of cognitive training benefits by integrating physiologic measures such as heart rate variability, galvanic skin responses, respiration rate, and EEG-based neural measures. These enhancements are intended to refine the adaptive mechanisms of neurogames, potentially leading to better therapeutic outcomes.
keyPoints: Integration of biosensors aims to enhance closed-loop mechanics for improved game adaptation.
Future neurogames will need rigorous clinical studies to validate their efficacy and generalizability.
Current evidence supports potential benefits in visual deficits (e.g., amblyopia) and cognitive remediation.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROGAMING

items.title: Resting-State Functional Connectivity (rsFC) as a Diagnostic Tool in Depression
items.content: Resting-state functional connectivity (rsFC) measures can be used to identify distinct neurophysiologic 'biotypes' in depressed patients, which are associated with different clinical-symptom profiles and predict responsiveness to treatments like TMS.
keyPoints: rsFC provides robust brain signals of energy consumption compared to task-based fMRI.
Drysdale et al. (2017) identified four neurophysiologic 'biotypes' in ~1000 depressed patients using rsFC.
These biotypes have distinct patterns of dysfunctional connectivity in limbic and frontostriatal networks.
Biotypes are associated with different symptom profiles such as anhedonia, anxiety, insomnia, and anergia.
rsFC-based biotyping has >80% diagnostic sensitivity and specificity for depression subtypes.
These biotypes can predict responsiveness to transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) therapy.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROIMAGING OF CONNECTIVITY

items.title: EEG and MEG as Complementary Tools in rsFC Studies
items.content: EEG and MEG provide cost-effective alternatives to fMRI for studying resting-state functional connectivity, with MEG offering superior spatial resolution and frequency range analysis.
keyPoints: EEG is the lowest-cost option but has limited spatial resolution.
MEG provides more accurate source-space estimates of functional oscillatory coupling than EEG.
MEG can measure activity at physiologically relevant frequencies up to 50 Hz, which are clinically useful.
fMRI studies slow activity fluctuations (<0.1 Hz), while EEG/MEG cover a broader frequency range.
The potential for EEG and MEG as diagnostic biomarkers in rsFC is underexploited and requires further research.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROIMAGING OF CONNECTIVITY

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Closed-Loop Neuroimaging in Neuropsychiatric Disorders
items.content: Closed-loop neuroimaging allows real-time manipulation of experiment parameters based on brain signals, offering therapeutic potential for neuropsychiatric disorders. It has been applied to chronic pain management, motor rehabilitation in Parkinson's and stroke patients, epilepsy, ADHD, and assessing consciousness in comatose patients.
keyPoints: Closed-loop neuroimaging enables real-time manipulation of experiment parameters based on brain signals.
Therapeutic applications include chronic pain, motor rehabilitation (Parkinson's, stroke), epilepsy, ADHD, and state-of-consciousness assessment in comatose patients.
Real-time fMRI, EEG, and MEG are commonly used methods.
Neurofeedback learning can modulate neural dynamics for therapeutic outcomes.
Mixed results in clinical RCTs due to individual heterogeneity in neuropsychiatric disorders.
Need for personalized therapies based on preintervention cognitive and neurophysiologic states.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · CLOSED-LOOP NEUROIMAGING

items.title: Challenges and Considerations in Implementing Closed-Loop Neuroimaging Therapies
items.content: While closed-loop neuroimaging shows promise, its clinical application faces challenges such as individual heterogeneity in neuropsychiatric disorders and the need for personalized approaches. Understanding learning principles and self-regulation mechanisms is crucial for effective neurofeedback.
keyPoints: Clinical RCTs show mixed results due to individual heterogeneity in neuropsychiatric disorders.
Therapies must be personalized based on preintervention cognitive and neurophysiologic states.
Understanding of learning principles and self-regulation mechanisms is essential for effective neurofeedback.
Practitioners need education on hardware/software capabilities of brain-computer interfaces.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · CLOSED-LOOP NEUROIMAGING

items.title: Mechanisms and Effects of TMS and tDCS in Neurological Disorders
items.content: TMS uses magnetic fields to induce electrical currents, while tDCS applies direct electrical currents at the scalp. Both methods aim to modulate brain activity, with TMS being painless and widely used. Animal studies suggest that anodal tDCS reduces resting membrane potential, whereas cathodal stimulation causes hyperpolarization. Prolonged tDCS can lead to enduring changes in cortical excitability.
keyPoints: TMS uses magnetic fields; tDCS uses direct electrical currents.
Anodal tDCS reduces resting membrane potential; cathodal causes hyperpolarization.
Prolonged tDCS can change cortical excitability.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.title: TMS and tDCS in Stroke Rehabilitation
items.content: TMS and tDCS are used to improve motor function post-stroke by modifying the balance of reciprocal inhibition between hemispheres. Low-frequency TMS inhibits the healthy cortex, while high-frequency stimulates the injured hemisphere. A meta-analysis showed significant benefits on motor outcomes, but a large trial (NICHE) found no long-term population-level benefit.
keyPoints: TMS and tDCS can improve motor function post-stroke.
Low-frequency TMS inhibits; high-frequency stimulates the injured hemisphere.
Meta-analysis shows significant benefits; NICHE trial showed no long-term benefit.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.title: TMS and tDCS in Psychiatric Disorders
items.content: TMS is supported for treating major depressive disorder (MDD) and is being investigated for PTSD, OCD, and schizophrenia. Different rTMS protocols (low-frequency ≤1 Hz and high-frequency 10–20 Hz) affect regional cerebral blood flow differently. EEG-guided TMS and tDCS targeting DLPFC imbalances are explored, but large RCTs have not shown benefits over placebo.
keyPoints: TMS is effective for MDD; investigated for PTSD, OCD, schizophrenia.
Low-frequency rTMS decreases blood flow; high-frequency increases it.
Large RCTs show no benefit over placebo.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.subtopic: IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION
items.title: Understanding the Mechanism and Clinical Impact of Cochlear Implants
items.content: Cochlear implants are sensory prostheses that restore hearing by converting environmental sounds into patterned stimulation delivered to the cochlear nerve. Over several months post-implantation, patients experience gradual improvements in speech perception due to activity-dependent sculpting of neural circuits.
keyPoints: Cochlear implants convert environmental sounds into patterned stimulation for the cochlear nerve.
Perceptual improvements occur gradually over several months after implantation.
Activity-dependent sculpting of neural circuits is hypothesized as the mechanism behind perceptual improvements.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION

items.subtopic: IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION
items.title: Responsive Stimulation in Refractory Focal Epilepsy: Efficacy and Risks
items.content: Responsive stimulation, an FDA-approved treatment for refractory focal epilepsy, uses closed-loop principles to detect early seizure signatures and deliver targeted electrical stimulation. A large RCT showed significant reduction in seizure frequency but not complete seizure freedom, with modest quality of life improvements and a small increased risk of hemorrhage.
keyPoints: Responsive stimulation is FDA-approved for refractory focal epilepsy.
It detects early seizure signatures and delivers targeted electrical stimulation.
RCT results: significant reduction in seizure frequency, rare seizure-free outcomes, modest QoL improvements, small increased hemorrhage risk.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION

items.subtopic: VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE
items.title: FDA-approved Vagus Nerve Stimulation (VNS) Enhances Motor Recovery Post-Stroke
items.content: Vagal nerve stimulation has been approved by the FDA to enhance motor recovery after stroke. Animal studies demonstrated safety and efficacy in enhancing plasticity, emphasizing the importance of precise timing during rehabilitation exercises. Clinical trials showed significant improvement in forelimb function with VNS.
keyPoints: FDA-approved therapy for post-stroke motor recovery
Animal studies show enhanced plasticity and safety
Precise timing of VNS during successful movement repetitions is crucial
VNS activates cholinergic and noradrenergic systems, improving attentional effects
Clinical trial: 6 weeks of VNS therapy with n = 53 participants showed significant forelimb function improvement in the VNS group compared to control
90 days post-study, a higher percentage of patients in the VNS group maintained clinically meaningful responses
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE

items.subtopic: VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE
items.title: Future Directions and Considerations for Vagus Nerve Stimulation Post-Stroke
items.content: While VNS is promising, variability in patient response necessitates further research to identify which stroke patients benefit most. Advances enabling home-based delivery of VNS could increase its utilization.
keyPoints: Variability in individual patient responses to VNS
Need for additional research to determine optimal candidates for VNS therapy
Potential for increased use with home-based VNS delivery
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE

items.title: BCIs as a Promising Solution for Severe Paralysis
items.content: Brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) offer a promising means to restore motor function in patients with severe paralysis due to various neurologic disorders. By directly communicating with the brain, BCIs can bypass disrupted pathways and translate neural activity into control signals for external devices.
keyPoints: BCIs aim to restore motor function by translating neural activity into control signals.
Effective for conditions like high cervical injuries, advanced ALS, and brainstem strokes where communication is severely impaired.
Components include recordings of neural activity, algorithms for signal transformation, an external device, and feedback mechanisms.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.title: Sources and Types of Neural Signals in BCIs
items.content: BCIs utilize various sources of neural signals to decode user intentions. Noninvasive EEG, invasive ECoG, spikes, and LFPs are key signal types used, each offering different resolutions for decoding.
keyPoints: EEG is noninvasive but offers lower resolution compared to invasive methods.
ECoG provides intermediate-resolution signals from the cortical surface.
Spikes (300–25,000 Hz) and LFPs (~0.1–300 Hz) are high- and low-bandwidth signals recorded via cortical penetration.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Feasibility and Communication Rates with BCIs
items.content: BCIs have demonstrated feasibility in clinical trials, enabling communication and control of assistive devices. Studies show that invasive neural recordings can achieve significant typing rates and word decoding capabilities.
keyPoints: Invasive BCIs have enabled brain-controlled typing at >30 characters per minute.
A locked-in ALS patient used an implantable BCI for communication via AAC device, achieving unsupervised use.
Recent studies using ECoG or spike-based decoding show potential for high-rate communication with large vocabularies.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.title: BCI Progress in Tetraplegia
items.content: Recent advancements in brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) have led to the development of commercial devices capable of recording spiking activity, with successful implementation in a participant with tetraplegia. This individual can control a computer cursor and play video games at home, demonstrating potential for practical applications despite challenges in long-term stability.
keyPoints: Progress in BCI translation has led to the development of commercial devices.
A participant with tetraplegia uses an implanted device to control a computer and play video games.
Challenges remain with long-term stability of neural interfaces.
Future efforts will focus on quantifying performance and targeting clinical needs based on patient-specific factors such as extent of disability.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 2)

items.title: Targeted Clinical Translational Efforts in BCI
items.content: As the characteristics and performance of BCIs become clearer, clinical efforts will increasingly focus on specific patient needs. This includes differentiating between high cervical injuries (above C4) and lower cervical injuries (below C5-C6), as well as tailoring interfaces for communication versus movement control.
keyPoints: Clinical translational efforts will be tailored to specific patient needs.
Differentiation is needed between high cervical injuries (above C4) and lower cervical injuries (below C5-C6).
Interfaces may vary based on whether they are intended for communication or movement control.
Anticipation of larger scale clinical studies over the next decade to demonstrate BCI capabilities in severe impairments.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY
items.question: Which of the following is a key goal of neurotherapeutic technologies?
options: To minimize the consequences of lost abilities, whether they are motor, sensory, or cognitive.
To completely reverse all effects of degenerative processes.
To eliminate the need for traditional medical care.
To reduce the necessity for high-intensity training regimens.
items.explanation: A key goal is to minimize the consequences of lost abilities, whether they are motor, sensory, or cognitive.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY

items.subtopic: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY
items.question: What has recent research suggested about the intensity of training for motor recovery after stroke?
options: It is less important than previously thought.
It may be particularly important for sustained long-term improvements.
It should be minimized to prevent overtraining.
It has no significant impact on functional outcomes.
items.explanation: Recent randomized controlled clinical trials have suggested that intensity of training may be particularly important for sustained long-term improvements.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY

items.subtopic: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY
items.question: What underlies observed functional improvements with rehabilitation according to studies in rodent and nonhuman primate models?
options: The reduction of cortical motor maps.
The plasticity of cortical 'motor maps' and coordinated firing of neurons in remaining networks.
The complete regeneration of lost neural pathways.
The elimination of neuroplasticity mechanisms.
items.explanation: Studies suggest that the plasticity of cortical 'motor maps' as well as the coordinated firing of neurons in remaining networks underlie observed functional improvements with rehabilitation.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY

items.question: What is a potential benefit of using robotic systems in motor rehabilitation protocols?
options: They can provide precise measurement and feedback on movement parameters.
They eliminate the need for any human intervention during therapy.
They are less costly compared to traditional therapies.
They completely replace conventional physical and occupational therapy.
items.explanation: Robotic systems are capable of precisely measuring movement parameters and providing quantitative feedback, which is a significant benefit in motor rehabilitation protocols.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · ROBOTICS

items.question: What challenge does the delivery of intensive training using robotic therapies face?
options: It can be difficult to implement and maintain outside of clinical trials.
Robotic systems are not capable of providing high-intensity movement practice.
Such training is only effective during the acute phase post-stroke.
Robotic therapy has been shown to worsen patient outcomes.
items.explanation: The delivery of intensive training using robotic therapies can be difficult to implement and maintain outside of clinical trials, posing a significant challenge.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · ROBOTICS

items.question: What is the role of antigravity support in upper limb rehabilitation robotics?
options: It allows practice and task engagement even with severe weakness.
It completely eliminates the need for conventional therapy.
It reduces the cost of robotic therapy significantly.
It prevents any form of fatigue during training sessions.
items.explanation: Antigravity support in upper limb rehabilitation robotics allows practice and task engagement even in the presence of severe weakness, which is challenging under current standards of care.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · ROBOTICS

items.question: Which component is essential for both virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR) therapeutic approaches?
options: Advanced hardware and computational methods to generate realistic perceptions
Use of only head-mounted viewers
Exclusive reliance on keyboard, mouse, or joystick interactions
Elimination of sensory feedback mechanisms
items.explanation: Both VR and AR rely on advanced hardware and computational methods to create simulated yet realistic perceptions.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.question: What is a unique advantage of using augmented reality (AR) in rehabilitation for stroke patients?
options: It completely replaces the need for physical therapy sessions.
It allows interactions with virtual objects only.
It can increase environmental enrichment, potentially improving motor and cognitive outcomes.
It eliminates the need for any form of sensory feedback.
items.explanation: AR may offer unique rehabilitation benefits by increasing environmental enrichment, which could improve motor and cognitive outcomes in stroke patients.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.question: What is a key difference between VR and AR as described in the passage?
options: VR overlays an artificial filter over real-world views.
AR allows interaction with a completely simulated reality.
VR enables interactions within a fully controlled simulated environment, while AR enhances actual physical world views.
Both VR and AR eliminate the need for tracking movements.
items.explanation: VR involves interacting in a fully simulated environment, whereas AR overlays artificial elements onto real-world views.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.question: How does augmented reality (AR) potentially benefit patients with spatial neglect after stroke?
options: By eliminating the need for any form of therapy.
By providing a completely virtual environment to train in.
By accounting for impaired modalities using AR methods.
By replacing all traditional rehabilitation techniques.
items.explanation: AR can be used to address spatial neglect by compensating for impaired sensory or motor functions.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.question: Which feature is essential for effective video game training in neurogaming?
options: Progressive adjustment of difficulty based on cognitive improvement
Static level of difficulty throughout the sessions
Focus solely on physical exertion metrics
Lack of user engagement mechanisms
items.explanation: Effective video game training requires progressive adjustment of difficulty in line with patient's cognitive improvement.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROGAMING

items.question: What is a primary area of active research in neurogaming?
options: Enhancing the visual appeal of games
Improving training transfer and sustainability over long periods
Reducing game complexity to make it easier for all users
Focusing solely on entertainment value
items.explanation: Research focuses on enhancing sustainability of neurogame training and improving the generalizability of task-specific training.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROGAMING

items.question: What is necessary to demonstrate definitive therapeutic benefit of neurogames?
options: Small-sample randomized controlled trials (RCTs)
Anecdotal evidence from users
Larger RCTs establishing neural target engagement and cognitive outcomes
Claims by the commercial brain training industry
items.explanation: Definitive therapeutic benefits require larger RCTs that establish neural target engagement and document cognitive and behavioral outcomes.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROGAMING

items.question: Which neuroimaging method provides more reliable brain signals of energy consumption than task-based fMRI approaches?
options: Resting-state functional connectivity (rsFC) using fMRI
EEG during specific cognitive tasks
Magnetoencephalography (MEG)
Task-based EEG
items.explanation: Resting-state functional connectivity (rsFC) using fMRI can provide more reliable brain signals of energy consumption than task-based fMRI approaches.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROIMAGING OF CONNECTIVITY

items.question: What is a major strength of MEG compared to EEG?
options: Lower cost than EEG
Higher spatial resolution than fMRI
More accurate source-space estimates of functional oscillatory coupling
Ability to study slow activity fluctuations
items.explanation: MEG provides more accurate source-space estimates of functional oscillatory coupling than EEG.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROIMAGING OF CONNECTIVITY

items.question: Which neuroimaging method is noted for its ability to study slow activity fluctuations?
options: EEG
MEG
fMRI
Task-based fMRI
items.explanation: fMRI can be used to study slow activity fluctuations (i.e., <0.1 Hz).
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROIMAGING OF CONNECTIVITY

items.question: Which neuroimaging method is NOT mentioned as being used in closed-loop neuroimaging therapeutic studies?
options: fMRI
EEG
MEG
PET
items.explanation: The passage mentions fMRI, EEG, and MEG but does not mention PET.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · CLOSED-LOOP NEUROIMAGING

items.question: What is a potential application of closed-loop neuroimaging-based neurofeedback for patients with ADHD?
options: Improvement of motor skills
Reduction of chronic pain
Enhancement of sustained attention
Deciphering state-of-consciousness
items.explanation: The passage states that closed-loop neuroimaging can improve cognitive abilities such as sustained attention in patients with ADHD.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · CLOSED-LOOP NEUROIMAGING

items.question: Why might clinical RCTs of closed-loop neuroimaging neurofeedback show mixed results?
options: Lack of advanced technology
Uniformity in neuropsychiatric disorders
Individual heterogeneity in neuropsychiatric disorders
Inadequate training for practitioners
items.explanation: The passage suggests that mixed results may be due to individual heterogeneity in neuropsychiatric disorders.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · CLOSED-LOOP NEUROIMAGING

items.question: Which noninvasive brain stimulation technique uses magnetic fields to generate electrical currents in the brain?
options: Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS)
Electroconvulsive therapy
Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS)
Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS)
items.explanation: TMS uses magnetic fields to generate electrical currents in the brain.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.question: What is a potential effect of anodal tDCS on cortical areas?
options: Hyperpolarization
Increased resting membrane potential
Generalized reduction in resting membrane potential
No change in resting membrane potential
items.explanation: Anodal tDCS causes a generalized reduction in resting membrane potential over large cortical areas.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.question: Which TMS paradigm is typically used to inhibit the healthy cortex after stroke?
options: High-frequency excitatory stimulation
Low-frequency inhibitory stimulation
Cathodal tDCS
Anodal tDCS
items.explanation: Low-frequency 'inhibitory' stimulation of the healthy cortex is used to modify the balance after stroke.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.question: What has been a challenge in demonstrating long-term benefits of TMS for motor recovery post-stroke?
options: Lack of FDA approval
Insufficient electrical field generation
Inability to modulate neural networks
Negative results from large multicenter trials
items.explanation: The NICHE trial did not find a benefit at the population level for long-term motor recovery post-stroke.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.subtopic: IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION
items.question: Which of the following is a hypothesized mechanism for perceptual improvements observed in patients with cochlear implants?
options: Activity-dependent sculpting of neural circuits
Immediate changes in cochlear nerve structure
Gradual increase in auditory cortex size
Enhanced synaptic transmission speed
items.explanation: Perceptual improvements are hypothesized to be due to activity-dependent sculpting of neural circuits.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION

items.subtopic: IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION
items.question: What is the principle behind responsive stimulation for refractory focal epilepsy?
options: Open-loop stimulation based on periodic assessments
Closed-loop stimulation based on real-time monitoring of brain oscillations
Randomized electrical stimulation to disrupt seizure activity
Continuous high-frequency stimulation regardless of brain state
items.explanation: Responsive stimulation is grounded on principles of closed-loop stimulation based on real-time monitoring of brain oscillations.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION

items.subtopic: IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION
items.question: What was a significant finding from the large RCT involving responsive stimulation in patients with intractable focal epilepsy?
options: All patients became seizure-free
No reduction in seizure frequency in the stimulation group
Significant reduction in seizure frequency, but rare for patients to become seizure-free
Increased risk of infection without any benefits
items.explanation: There was a significant reduction in seizure frequency in the stimulation group, but it was rare for patients to become seizure-free.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION

items.subtopic: VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE
items.question: Which systems are rapidly activated by vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) to potentially enhance attentional effects and improve 'signal to noise'?
options: Dopaminergic and serotonergic
Cholinergic and noradrenergic
GABAergic and glutamatergic
Opioid and endocannabinoid
items.explanation: VNS results in rapid activation of cholinergic and noradrenergic systems, which may enhance attentional effects.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE

items.subtopic: VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE
items.question: What is the importance of timing when using vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) in stroke rehabilitation?
options: It should be administered continuously without regard to movement
Timing is irrelevant as long as VNS is used
Precise timing during successful movements enhances efficacy
Stimulation should occur before any movement attempts
items.explanation: The precise timing of VNS, particularly at the end of successful movement repetitions, is essential for its efficacy.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE

items.subtopic: VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE
items.question: What was a significant outcome observed in participants randomized to the VNS group in the pivotal trial?
options: Decreased forelimb function compared to control
No difference in forelimb function compared to control
Significant increase in forelimb function compared to control
Immediate recovery of all stroke-related deficits
items.explanation: Participants randomized to the VNS group had a significant increase in forelimb function compared to the control group.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a source of neural signals used in brain-computer interfaces?
options: EEG
ECoG
Spikes
MRI
items.explanation: MRI is not mentioned as a source of neural signals for BCIs; EEG, ECoG, and spikes are.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.question: What type of BCI allows a subject's intention to move to be translated in real time to control an external device?
options: Sensory BCI
Motor BCI
Cognitive BCI
Emotional BCI
items.explanation: A 'motor' BCI translates a subject's intention to move into control signals for an external device.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is true about ECoG in comparison to EEG and spikes/LFPs?
options: ECoG has higher resolution than spikes/LFPs
ECoG signals are noninvasive
ECoG provides intermediate-resolution signals
ECoG can only be used for sensory BCIs
items.explanation: ECoG is described as providing an intermediate-resolution signal compared to spikes/LFPs and EEG.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.question: What was demonstrated by early pilot clinical trials of BCIs based on invasive recordings?
options: BCIs are ineffective for communication
High rates of brain-controlled typing are possible
Noninvasive methods are superior to invasive ones
BCIs can only be used with nonhuman primates
items.explanation: Early pilot clinical trials showed that relatively high rates of brain-controlled typing (>30 characters per minute) are possible.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which participant group has a first-in-class commercial BCI device implanted for recording spiking activity?
options: Individuals with lower cervical injuries
Participants with severe impairments in communication
A single participant with tetraplegia
Patients with high cervical injuries
items.explanation: The passage mentions a single participant with tetraplegia implanted with the device.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 2)

items.question: What is one of the challenges mentioned regarding long-term use of neural interfaces?
options: The need for larger scale clinical studies
Quantifying stability and performance levels
Differentiating between communication and movement control needs
Scaling findings to a commercially viable device
items.explanation: Challenges with long-term stability include quantifying how stable neural interfaces are.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 2)

items.question: What is anticipated over the next decade regarding BCIs?
options: BCIs will be limited to noninvasive applications
Clinical studies will demonstrate BCI capabilities for communication and assistive technology control
Commercial efforts will cease due to technological limitations
All patients with cervical injuries will receive the same type of interface
items.explanation: Larger scale clinical studies are anticipated to show how BCIs enable communication and control of assistive technology.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY
items.statement: The brain remains highly plastic regardless of age and even in the face of ongoing injury and/or degenerative processes.
items.explanation: It is stated that the brain remains highly plastic regardless of age and even in the face of ongoing injury and/or degenerative processes.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY

items.subtopic: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY
items.statement: Neurologic rehabilitation does not aim to harness activity-dependent plasticity mechanisms.
items.explanation: Neurologic rehabilitation aims to harness activity-dependent plasticity mechanisms to maximize functional restoration.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY

items.statement: Robotic therapy has been shown to provide outcomes that are worse than existing standards of care.
items.explanation: Recent analysis suggests that robotic devices may at least match outcomes realized with existing standards of care, not worsen them.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · ROBOTICS

items.statement: Rehabilitation robotics can provide more precise feedback and support novel quantitative rehabilitation approaches.
items.explanation: Robotic systems are capable of providing precise feedback and supporting novel quantitative rehabilitation methods, enhancing the recovery process.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · ROBOTICS

items.statement: Virtual reality (VR) environments are designed to maximize patient engagement and motivation through tailored experiences.
items.explanation: The passage states that VR can be tailored to individual needs, maximizing engagement and motivation.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.statement: Augmented reality (AR) completely replaces the physical world with a virtual one.
items.explanation: AR overlays artificial elements onto the real-world view, rather than replacing it entirely.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.statement: Neurogames have been shown to improve speed of processing in older adults.
items.explanation: Neurogames target speed of processing and multitasking abilities, showing benefits for aging.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROGAMING

items.statement: The commercial brain training industry's claims about neurogaming have been validated by the scientific community.
items.explanation: Claims made by the commercial brain training industry have been dismissed by the scientific community.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROGAMING

items.statement: EEG provides the highest spatial resolution among neuroimaging methods.
items.explanation: EEG compromises on spatial resolution compared to other methods like fMRI and MEG.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROIMAGING OF CONNECTIVITY

items.statement: rsFC measures have been used to predict responsiveness to transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) therapy.
items.explanation: rsFC measures can predict responsiveness to TMS therapy, as shown in studies with depressed patients.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROIMAGING OF CONNECTIVITY

items.statement: Closed-loop neuroimaging methods can only advance our understanding of dynamic brain function and have no therapeutic potential.
items.explanation: The passage states that closed-loop imaging has both scientific and therapeutic potential.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · CLOSED-LOOP NEUROIMAGING

items.statement: Neurofeedback learning can be applied as therapy for motor rehabilitation in Parkinson's patients.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that neurofeedback learning has been used for motor rehabilitation in Parkinson's and stroke patients.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · CLOSED-LOOP NEUROIMAGING

items.statement: TMS is known to be painful and therefore less commonly used in NIBS.
items.explanation: TMS is known to be painless, which contributes to its widespread use for NIBS.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.statement: Recent research indicates that typical stimulation paradigms may not generate sufficient electrical fields to modulate neural activity.
items.explanation: There is uncertainty about how much current can penetrate the skull and modulate neural networks.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.subtopic: IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION
items.statement: Deep-brain stimulation (DBS) is commonly thought to function by activating the targeted neural areas.
items.explanation: For movement disorders, it is commonly thought that targeted areas are functionally inhibited by the chronic electrical stimulation.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION

items.subtopic: IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION
items.statement: Responsive stimulation for epilepsy involves delivering focused electrical stimulation after seizure onset.
items.explanation: The device aims to detect the earliest signatures of the onset of a seizure and then deliver focused electrical stimulation to prevent further progression.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · IMPLANTABLE NEURAL INTERFACES | IMPLANTABLE DEVICES FOR NEUROMODULATION

items.subtopic: VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE
items.statement: Vagal nerve stimulation (VNS) has been approved by the FDA as a therapy for enhancing motor recovery after stroke.
items.explanation: The FDA recently approved VNS as a therapy to enhance motor recovery post-stroke.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE

items.subtopic: VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE
items.statement: All stroke patients are likely to benefit equally from vagus nerve stimulation (VNS).
items.explanation: The variability of effects for single patients necessitates further research to determine which stroke patients will most benefit.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE

items.statement: BCIs can restore function by communicating directly with the muscles.
items.explanation: BCIs communicate directly with the brain, not muscles.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Decoding a large vocabulary using phoneme-based decoding is possible with spike-based recordings.
items.explanation: A study indicated that decoding of a large vocabulary was possible using phoneme-based decoding with spike-based recordings.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.statement: BCI devices have been scaled to a commercially viable device for multiple participants.
items.explanation: The passage states there is only one participant with a commercial BCI device implanted.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with high cervical injuries have different rehabilitation needs compared to those with lower cervical injuries.
items.explanation: The passage specifies that patients with high cervical injuries have different needs than those with lower cervical injuries.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY
items.assertion: Clinicians have the most knowledge and experience about specific disease processes, available treatments, and the expected course of illnesses affecting the nervous system.
items.reason: It is thus critical that neurologic specialists and other clinicians play an important role in the future adoption of these technologies for neurologic rehabilitation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why clinicians' involvement is crucial.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies | NONINVASIVE TECHNOLOGIES TO HARNESS PLASTICITY

items.assertion: Augmented reality (AR) can be used to enhance visual feedback during gait training.
items.reason: Both AR and VR are capable of enhancing sensory feedback, which is important for rehabilitation exercises like gait training.
items.explanation: The assertion is true because the passage mentions that both AR and VR can be used to enhance visual feedback during gait training.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.assertion: Mirror therapy for phantom limb pain requires movement of the unaffected contralateral limb.
items.reason: AR-based therapy allows movements based on the remaining portion of the affected limb, unlike traditional mirror therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is true and the reason correctly explains that AR can use the affected limb for therapy, differentiating it from mirror therapy.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VIRTUAL AND AUGMENTED REALITY

items.assertion: Resting-state functional connectivity (rsFC) is a robust, task-independent measure of brain function.
items.reason: Methodologic research has shown that rs-fMRI can provide more reliable brain signals than specific task-based fMRI approaches.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why rsFC is considered robust.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NEUROIMAGING OF CONNECTIVITY

items.assertion: Closed-loop neuroimaging-based therapies need to be personalized.
items.reason: This is because there is no one-size-fits-all therapy due to individual heterogeneity in neuropsychiatric disorders.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · CLOSED-LOOP NEUROIMAGING

items.assertion: TMS has been approved by the FDA for treating depression.
items.reason: There is a substantial body of evidence supporting TMS as an antidepressant in major depressive disorder (MDD).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why TMS is approved for treating depression.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.assertion: High-frequency rTMS decreases regional cerebral blood flow.
items.reason: High-frequency rTMS elicits increased blood flow over the prefrontal region and associated circuits.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; high-frequency rTMS increases, not decreases, regional cerebral blood flow.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · NONINVASIVE BRAIN STIMULATION

items.subtopic: VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE
items.assertion: Future advances in VNS delivery could lead to greater use of this therapy.
items.reason: Allowing VNS to be delivered in the home setting will facilitate its broader adoption.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why future advances may increase the use of VNS.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · VAGUS NERVE STIMULATION TO IMPROVE RECOVERY AFTER STROKE

items.assertion: BCIs have shown potential in restoring communication for patients with severe impairments.
items.reason: Studies using ECoG or spike-based decoding demonstrated successful communication restoration.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: EEG signals require invasive placement of electrodes.
items.reason: EEG is a noninvasive method for obtaining neural signals.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as EEG does not require invasive electrode placement; the reason is true.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: BCIs are expected to provide targeted clinical translational efforts based on specific patient needs.
items.reason: As the characteristics of neural interfaces become clearer, it will allow for more tailored approaches.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why BCIs can be targeted to specific patient needs.
items.reference: Emerging Neurotherapeutic Technologies · BRAIN-COMPUTER INTERFACES FOR PARALYSIS (part 2)
